summary: MiMeNet uses a multi-layer perceptron neural network (MLPNN) to model the community metabolome profile based on microbiome samples.
MiMeNet benefits from multivariate learning by predicting the abundance of all metabolites at once, which facilitates prediction through shared information across different metabolites.
MiMeNet uses biclustering to organize microbes and metabolites into modules based on their feature attribution patterns.
MiMeNet identified 8 modules of microbes and 8 modules of metabolites in the IBD (PRISM) dataset.
Four metabolite modules were enriched in healthy subjects in the IBD study.
Three metabolite modules were enriched in IBD patients.
Module 2 (healthy-enriched) contained medium-chain fatty acids, triterpenoids, and cholesterols.

Module 7 (healthy-enriched) was mainly composed of short-chain fatty acids such as propionate, butyrate, and valeric acid. Short-chain fatty acids (propionate, butyrate, and valeric acid) have been shown to be protective against IBD.
Five butyrate-producing microbes (Eubacterium biforme, Eubacterium hallii, Eubacterium rectale, Roseburia intestinalis, and Roseburia inulinivorans) were found in microbial module 6.
MiMeNet's modules capture biologically meaningful microbe-metabolite interactions by grouping microbes with similar functional effects on metabolites.
